problem: When building an observability hook to capture Python warnings (RuntimeWarning, DeprecationWarning, etc.), wrapping warnings.showwarning directly fails because other libraries (Torch, langfuse, OpenTelemetry, pytest) can replace the global showwarning handler at startup, breaking the chain if they don't call the previous handler. Result: warning capture goes silent in production even though it works locally.
solution: Use logging.captureWarnings(True) instead of wrapping warnings.showwarning. This routes ALL warnings to the 'py.warnings' logger via the standard library mechanism, immune to third-party replacements. Attach your filter to the 'py.warnings' logger only (not to handlers or root) to avoid duplicate stamps. Note: record.filename will be 'warnings.py' (the captureWarnings shim), so extract the actual source from the formatted message using regex like r'^(\S+?):(\d+):' on record.getMessage(). Check 'if my_filter not in logger.filters' before addFilter() for idempotency since captureWarnings(True) itself is idempotent.
error_signature: warning capture hook stops working in production; warnings.showwarning replaced by third-party library

problem: Chat task worker marks tasks as successful based on output length (>= 20 chars) without verifying that requested deliverables (files, commits, artifacts) actually exist. When file-path validation fails silently and the model enters a recovery loop of failed tool calls followed by brief lead-in text ('Let me try...'), the worker incorrectly reports success=True despite no substantive work being completed.
solution: 1. Pass absolute file paths to chat-task workers instead of relative paths, since relative path validation may fail against the backend working directory. 2. After chat-tasks that should produce artifacts (new files, git commits, vault entries), verify the artifact actually exists before trusting success=True. Check with git log --since, file existence checks, or directory listing. 3. For a permanent fix: replace the output-length heuristic in the chat-task-worker success check with either (a) requiring explicit deliverable declaration in task content + post-run validation, (b) pattern detection for 'lead-in only' output (repeated short text blocks + tool_use with no synthesis), or (c) model self-verification in a final turn asking 'did you complete X?'.
error_signature: chat_task_worker: success=True with output>20chars but zero artifact delivery; File not found on relative paths; silent tool failure + model lead-in loop

problem: When resuming multi-session development work, uncommitted changes in the working tree from prior sessions can be silently load-bearing on production. For example: missing dependencies in package.json/package-lock.json that were imported in committed code, event listeners or initialization code in Vue components not yet staged, or build config overrides. A clean deploy or rebuild can fail or break because these changes were never committed, even though prior commits depended on them. Node module caches can hide missing dependencies until a purge forces a clean install.
solution: Before committing new work in a multi-session project: (1) Run 'git status -s' to list all modified and untracked files. (2) For each modified file, run 'git diff HEAD -- <path>' and determine whether the last committed code would break without that change. (3) If yes, stage and commit it as a prerequisite (do not amend already-shipped commits). (4) Group logically-related fixes with the prior commit's scope (e.g., 'fix(feature-name): ...'). Separate unrelated UX polish into its own commit. (5) Assume build caches are unreliable; a clean install after deploy may expose missing dependencies not caught during cached builds.
error_signature: Production fails on clean deploy; missing dependency error or broken event listener after uncommitted changes were left in working tree

problem: Cloudflare dashboard 'Create application' button now defaults to Workers Builds UI instead of classic Pages flow (as of ~2026-04). Static-only sites (Astro, Vite, Next-export, Hugo, Jekyll) deployed via Workers Builds without wrangler.toml fail with errors like 'no main entry / no [assets]' or 'Deploy command: npx wrangler deploy' with no framework detection or build output directory fields.
solution: For static-only sites, use the classic Pages flow instead: navigate to dashboard -> Workers & Pages -> Create application, then look for a separate 'Pages' option at the 'Select a method' step (may be a tab, card, or 'Other deployment methods' link). Classic Pages auto-detects framework and serves dist/ without wrangler config. If Pages flow is unavailable, add a minimal wrangler.toml with: name = "project-name", compatibility_date = "YYYY-MM-DD", and [assets] directory = "./dist" section. Note: classic Pages uses *.pages.dev URLs while Workers Builds uses *.workers.dev, but site behavior is identical. Classic Pages requires no additional config for static sites and is simpler than Workers Builds for that use case.
error_signature: Deploy command: npx wrangler deploy / no main entry / no [assets] / Workers Builds static site deployment

problem: Long-lived Python daemon processes cache bytecode at startup. After git pull updates source files, running daemons continue executing old bytecode from memory. The fix appears to be deployed (file shows corrected code, fresh Python processes load it), but existing daemons log old behavior indefinitely. Symptoms: persistent errors despite on-disk patches, discrepancies between ad-hoc probes and daemon logs, log message wording mismatches with current source, errors stopping at times unrelated to restarts you initiated.
solution: Identify which daemon processes hold stale bytecode by comparing process start time (from ps lstart) against modification times of files in that process's import surface. Restart stale daemons using launchctl kickstart. To automate: compute the diff between a prior deploy ref and HEAD, map changed files to the daemons that import them (backend imports backend/, zugamind imports zugamind/ plus core.cost.*, core.database.*, core.identity.*, domains.cognition.*; zugabee imports only zugabee/; per-studio daemons import their own directory plus ZugaCore/), then kickstart those LaunchAgents. Verify the fix loaded by spawning a fresh Python process and importing the changed module; if fresh import works but daemon logs show old behavior, the daemon restart is still needed.
error_signature: Fixed code on disk but daemon continues old behavior; ad-hoc probe shows fix, daemon logs show bug

problem: When syncing a fork with its canonical upstream repository, developers often assume the canonical is always ahead and current, leading to blindly pulling fork changes forward. This can accidentally promote bugs or unintended code (like privacy leaks or debug endpoints) from the fork into the canonical, which then propagates to all consumers.
solution: Always run a diff (e.g., `diff --strip-trailing-cr -u canonical fork`) and manually review every hunk BEFORE deciding sync direction. For each difference, ask: is this real work that should be promoted, or drift/bugs that should be deleted? Do not trust file size, line count, or commit count as signals of value. Strip trailing CR/LF noise first. Remember that 'fork is ahead' is a neutral statement -- it could mean valuable work or rejected upstream bugs. Diffs resolve the ambiguity; assumptions do not.
error_signature: accidental merge of fork bugs into canonical repository; unintended promotion of debug endpoints or privacy leaks

problem: When a repository or config file mentions a hostname, it is easy to assume the service deploys to that hostname. However, the hostname could be a DNS redirect, proxy entry, frontend fetch target, or be served by a completely different service. This can lead to incorrect memory claims about which service runs at which hostname, causing future deployment attempts to fail or deploy to the wrong location.
solution: Before writing memory that claims a service runs at a specific hostname, perform a read-only HTTP probe to verify. Run two requests: (1) curl -sS https://<host>/openapi.json | head -c 300 to check if a FastAPI service self-identifies with the expected name, and (2) curl -sS https://<host>/api/health/live to check the service status endpoint. Record the HTTP response status and a 20-character snippet of the body as evidence. If the hostname's /openapi.json title or health endpoint does not match the expected service name, the repo likely does not deploy there. This prevents false inferences and catches cases where git evidence (commit messages, config files) disagrees with live HTTP evidence.
error_signature: assuming repo mentions hostname => repo deploys to hostname (drift between git metadata and live service identity)

problem: Docker layer caching causes stale upstream dependencies when building a Dockerfile that clones multiple git repositories in a single RUN layer. Pushing changes to an upstream repo and rebuilding the image results in the old cloned code being reused, with no visible error -- the container appears healthy but runs outdated code. Symptom: routes/imports that were deleted from upstream still respond; OpenAPI schema shows old endpoints; code greps confirm stale files are present in the built image.
solution: Docker's layer cache is keyed by the exact RUN command text. When git clone commands are chained in one RUN layer, Docker reuses that entire layer if nothing in the command string changes, ignoring new upstream commits. To force a rebuild: (1) Identify the cache-busting variable in use (e.g., CACHE_BUST ARG in the Dockerfile). (2) Check if a service-level environment variable with the same name overrides the Dockerfile default -- on platforms like Railway, service variables take precedence over ARG defaults and are passed as build-args. Verify with `<deployment-tool> variables | grep CACHE_BUST`. (3) Update the cache-bust variable to a new unique value (e.g., v719 -> v720, with a short descriptive suffix like 'upstream-repos-fresh'). This new value must be set at the place where it is actually used: if a service-level variable exists, update it there (e.g., `railway variables --set CACHE_BUST=v720-reason`); if only Dockerfile ARG is in use, update the ARG default there. (4) Rebuild and verify the change took effect by checking build logs for the new cache-bust value and comparing image digests before/after (if digests are identical, the layer was reused and the cache-bust did not work). (5) Validate the actual code change landed by grepping files inside the container or checking generated artifacts like API schemas.
error_signature: stale code in docker image after upstream push; docker layer cache hit despite upstream changes; deleted routes/imports still present after rebuild; identical image digests on consecutive builds

problem: When a feature is rebuilt in a better code location, leaving the old implementation in place causes silent data drift. The old code path remains importable and syntactically valid, but writes migrate to the new location while reads stay split between old and new. This is invisible to grep-by-import or code linting -- only mtime inspection on data directories reveals the zombie writes. Example: a codebase has both memory_stats code reading from flat root directory and remember_* functions writing to nested FILE_MAP structure; they silently diverge until read failures surface weeks later.
solution: Same-day deletion rule when completing a migration: (1) Identify the old and new code paths. (2) Before deleting, verify the type of duplicate: hand-copied files (use rm -rf), git submodule (use git submodule deinit && git rm), or symlink (use rm). Run git ls-tree to disambiguate -- mode 160000 means submodule, 040000 tree means directory, 100644 blob means file. (3) Redirect all callers to the new path and merge tests. (4) Delete the old path same-day; do not defer cleanup. (5) For systems with many external callers (>5 touchpoints), use collapse-to-leader instead: make new code canonical (owns the state machine), make old code a thin read-only view over the new side's published events. Retain backup copies of old code 7 days for rollback. (6) To audit an existing codebase for this pattern, check mtimes on data directories -- uniform timestamps across many files suggest bulk restore/sync and indicate dormant code paths.
error_signature: file not found on read path after writes redirect to new location; stale files in old directory while new directory shows recent mtimes; dual implementations writing to similar but divergent storage paths

problem: When reconciling dual-machine git state (especially Windows vs Mac), a large dirty tree can be caused by EOL/whitespace phantom changes rather than real content drift. Without a triage signal, operators either over-preserve by saving work-in-progress branches of pure whitespace noise, or under-preserve by fast-forwarding through real content changes that happen to have whitespace-heavy churn.
solution: Run both 'git diff --stat HEAD' (raw delta) and 'git diff --ignore-all-space --stat HEAD' (content delta). Calculate ratio = (ignore_ws_insertions + ignore_ws_deletions) / (raw_insertions + raw_deletions). Use ratio thresholds to classify: 0-20% = EOL phantom dominant (safe to fast-forward); 20-50% with origin overlap = likely superseded (preserve wip-branch as backup); >50% with coherent theme = real WIP (defer to human). Always spot-check one representative file before deciding. Note: exclude binary files from ratio calculation; check 'git submodule status' separately if submodule pointers changed.
error_signature: large git dirty tree with unclear cause (Windows CRLF vs Mac LF); git diff --stat shows N changed lines but actual content unclear

problem: Electron app release/auto-update fails with 403 'Resource not accessible by personal access token' when using electron-builder --publish with a fine-grained PAT that lacks Contents:write scope on the target repository.
solution: Use gh CLI (which has repo scope) instead of electron-builder --publish. Build installers locally with npm run package and npm run package:ow, then upload via: gh release create v<version> dist/Ludus-<version>-*Setup.exe dist/ow.yml dist/latest.yml -R <owner>/<repo> --latest. Ensure version.yml/latest.yml manifests (containing sha512 hashes) are regenerated by the build and uploaded alongside executables, since the app's autoUpdater reads GitHub releases and validates installer integrity against these manifests. If using a wrapper release script, hardcode the gh path and disable electron-builder publish flags to prevent accidental fallback to the broken PAT approach.
error_signature: electron-builder publish 403 Resource not accessible by personal access token | autoUpdater fails to validate installer against manifest

problem: SQLAlchemy recreates dropped database tables on backend startup. Using DROP TABLE alone does not permanently remove a table because Base.metadata.create_all() is called during initialization and re-creates any tables whose model classes are still defined.
solution: To permanently remove a table from a SQLAlchemy database: (1) Delete the model class definition from the ORM models file. (2) Remove the model export from the models __init__.py file. (3) Execute DROP TABLE via direct database client. (4) Restart the application. The table will not be recreated because Base.metadata.create_all() will no longer find the model definition. Before dropping, backup the database file. After restart, verify the table is gone by listing all tables -- if it reappeared, search the entire codebase for remaining model class definitions.
error_signature: DROP TABLE succeeded but table reappears after application restart; Base.metadata.create_all() recreates missing tables for all registered models

problem: Vite HMR (hot-reload) breaks audio pipeline in Electron apps with IPC audio routing. After hot-reload, AudioContext, analyser nodes, and audio processors fail to reconnect to the main process. ScriptProcessor -> IPC -> main process audio routing survives in the main process but the renderer's audio state becomes stale and disconnected.
solution: When developing Electron apps with audio that routes through IPC to the main process, do not rely on Vite HMR for testing. Instead, perform a full dev server restart: kill the dev port (e.g. `npx kill-port 5173`) and run your dev command again (e.g. `npm run dev`). This ensures both renderer and main process audio state is synchronized. The issue occurs because the Electron main process retains its state across HMR, but the renderer is refreshed, breaking the audio pipeline handshake.
error_signature: AudioContext not reconnecting after HMR; analyser nodes stale; wake word engine unresponsive post-hot-reload

problem: Playwright's browser_snapshot() function extracts the accessibility tree as text, which includes the plaintext values of <input type="password"> fields when they are autofilled by the browser's password manager. This causes stored passwords to appear in tool output, logs, and transcripts as literal text.
solution: On login pages or pages that may contain autofilled credentials: (1) Use browser_take_screenshot() instead of browser_snapshot() to capture the page as an image, avoiding text extraction entirely. (2) If snapshot is necessary for element references, understand that any credential field values will be exposed and plan to rotate those credentials afterward. (3) When snapshotting authenticated pages, manually scan the returned YAML output for password-type inputs and any other sensitive fields (API keys in hidden inputs, session tokens in value attributes) before using the snapshot.
error_signature: password exposed in browser_snapshot accessibility tree; plaintext value in tool output

problem: After renaming a folder in a Python project, runtime artifacts that are not git-tracked break silently. Specifically: hardcoded sibling directory paths in constants become stale, leftover state files (PAUSE flags, lock files) are forgotten, required directories are never created on fresh clones causing ENOENT on first write, and data files in the old location are orphaned.
solution: Within 24 hours of a folder rename, audit these 5 categories: (1) Search for _DEFAULT_*_DIR and _DIR = constants that use Path(__file__).resolve().parent / '<sibling>' — update any pointing to moved sibling directories by changing to parent.parent / new_sibling_name. (2) Check for leftover state files with 'find . -name PAUSE' and 'find . -name *.lock' in the renamed dirs; remove any from incomplete migration steps. (3) Identify empty-but-required directories (like analytics/, receipts/, logs/) that should exist; add .gitkeep files to them so they are created on fresh clones. (4) Search for the old folder name in cron jobs and launchctl plist files; update paths to the new folder name. (5) Locate JSONL and sqlite data files in the old location; move them to the new location with explicit mv commands rather than relying on auto-recreation. Use grep and find commands to identify all instances before runtime errors surface days later.
error_signature: ENOENT on write to newly-created directory; silent None returns from stale hardcoded paths; leftover PAUSE/lock files causing daemon to skip work; orphaned data files not auto-recreated

problem: Emails sent via Resend to AOL, Yahoo, or iCloud recipients are silently spam-foldered or dropped on first contact, even though the send endpoint returns success and Gmail delivery works fine. Root cause: SPF record is missing the amazonses.com include (Resend runs on AWS SES), causing SPF to fail alignment at receiving MTAs.
solution: When configuring a domain to send via Resend, ensure the SPF TXT record includes both your existing inbound-routing include AND 'include:amazonses.com'. Verify with: dig +short TXT <domain>. Also publish a DMARC record (start with p=none; v=DMARC1; rua=<admin-email>) at _dmarc.<domain>. Verify with: dig +short TXT _dmarc.<domain>. Publish DKIM at resend._domainkey.<domain>. Only one SPF v=spf1 record per domain name is allowed (RFC 7208) -- merge all includes into a single TXT record. Test delivery to at least one real AOL or Yahoo address to confirm, since Gmail's filters are much more permissive than strict providers and may mask delivery problems.
error_signature: email sent successfully but not delivered to AOL/Yahoo/iCloud inboxes; arrives in spam folder or missing; works fine to Gmail

problem: When root-level stub files are deleted from a shared memory vault structure, some are auto-regenerated by the memory system even though their canonical content exists in subfolders. Specifically, deleting _index.md at the vault root causes retrieve_memory() calls without a category parameter to fail with 'Memory file not found', breaking user-facing functionality.
solution: Understand that this memory system has a two-level file structure: root-level stubs (generated from templates) and canonical subfolder files (actual data storage). Do not delete root stub files that match template keys (preferences.md, codebase_notes.md, technical_decisions.md, patterns.md, active_work.md, mistakes.md, _index.md) because they auto-regenerate and _index.md is hard-coded as the entry point in retrieval.py:63. The canonical layer for reads/writes is the subfolder path defined in FILE_MAP (retrieval.py:26-36), not the root stubs. If performing vault cleanup, verify which files are load-bearing by checking the retrieval.py hard-coded paths before deletion.
error_signature: Memory file not found when calling retrieve_memory() without category parameter; root stub files auto-regenerate after deletion despite subfolder equivalents existing

problem: When a cloud deployment (e.g., Railway) shows FAILED status, the build error is not immediately visible. The deployment may appear to work from outside because a previous successful container continues serving traffic, masking the actual build failure. Additionally, local builds may succeed while CI/git-based builds fail if source files are untracked in version control but present on the local filesystem.
solution: 1. On a FAILED deployment, immediately pull build logs using the deployment ID (e.g., 'railway logs --build <id>') and read the last 20-30 lines for the actual error. Do not assume a healthcheck pass or retry without inspecting logs first. 2. Before deploying, audit untracked files in the changed code surface ('git status -s') to identify files that exist locally but are not committed. Any '??' files in the upload path will not exist in git-sourced builds. 3. Fix the build error, redeploy, and commit all necessary source files together to keep local and git-based build paths synchronized. 4. When consecutive deploys fail with the same error, stop and diagnose from logs rather than retrying with variations (e.g., cache-bust tweaks). 5. Read the full build log, not just the final exit summary -- look for '[ERRO]' or 'error during build:' prefixes which may appear 20+ lines before the end.
error_signature: FAILED deployment status; healthcheck passed but actual build error; untracked files cause local-build-succeeds-but-git-build-fails divergence

problem: When using SuperTokens middleware in a FastAPI application, the auth middleware runs before route resolution. An unauthenticated request to a non-existent /api/* route returns 200 (SPA fallback serving index.html) or 401 (if auth middleware intercepts), making it impossible to distinguish between a route that doesn't exist and one that requires authentication.
solution: Use two probes to determine route existence: (1) Baseline probe an obviously fake path like /api/DOESNOTEXIST and note its response code and body. (2) Probe your target path. If it matches the baseline response, the route does not exist (SPA fallback). If it differs (e.g., 401 or different body), the route exists. For authoritative verification, query the OpenAPI schema at /openapi.json and grep the paths object: curl -s https://example.com/openapi.json | python -c 'import sys, json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print([p for p in d["paths"] if "/api/target" in p])'. If the path is absent from the schema, the route does not exist regardless of HTTP responses.
error_signature: 401 vs 200 ambiguity on non-existent /api/* routes with auth middleware and SPA fallback

problem: UI elements render blank, transparent, or missing glyphs despite no compile errors. Two common causes: (1) A parent store or utility claims to re-export a field via computed() but the field is missing from the return block — causes undefined at runtime and silent component blanking. (2) CSS custom properties (var(--name)) reference tokens defined in an imported stylesheet, but that stylesheet was never imported in the main CSS entrypoint — vars fall back silently and elements lose color/visibility.
solution: When a UI element appears empty or unstyled: (1) Open dev tools Console immediately and search for 'Unhandled error during execution of render function' or 'Cannot read properties of undefined' — this pinpoints the missing field name and source line in ~5 seconds. (2) For CSS-only issues (no console error), inspect the element's computed styles and look for unresolved var(--name) declarations. Search the design-tokens source directory for where the variable is defined, then verify that file is actually imported in your main CSS entrypoint (e.g., src/assets/main.css). (3) For store-level issues, confirm that any field accessed via store.X actually appears in both the computed() getter AND the return block of useFooStore. (4) Do not assume Electron window mode, Vite hash caching, dev-vs-prod differences, or HMR issues until wiring is ruled out — wiring gaps are the most common silent failure in this pattern. (5) When you find a missing CSS import, audit all related component stylesheets at once (button.css, input.css, modal.css, etc. often ship together in design-token packages) and add them in a single edit.
error_signature: blank UI element OR missing icon/glyph + no build error OR console shows 'Cannot read properties of undefined' OR 'var(--name)' resolves to fallback color

problem: When a service validates JWT tokens via JWKS and then calls a downstream API that uses a separate user database, the JWT's 'sub' claim (from the auth provider) may not match the API's internal user ID. Calling the API with the wrong ID silently creates orphaned records keyed on the auth provider's ID, causing spurious authorization failures ('insufficient balance', etc.) even when the user has legitimate access.
solution: After JWT validation extracts the 'sub' claim, resolve it to the API's internal user ID before calling protected endpoints. Call GET /api/auth/me with the Bearer token to obtain {id, email, role, ...} keyed on the API's internal ID scheme. Cache the (sub, exp) -> (internal_id, email) mapping for the token's lifetime to avoid redundant round-trips. Pass the internal_id (not the auth provider's sub) and email to downstream API calls. Reference: implement a simple in-memory dict resolver that checks expiration before reuse (~60 lines).
error_signature: API returns permission denied or zero balance despite user having valid access; orphaned records appear in logs keyed on unexpected user ID format

problem: Symlinks created on one OS (Windows, macOS, Linux) and copied to another machine silently break because they store absolute or relative paths that don't exist on the target system. Tools like Vite serve 404s with no console error, making debugging extremely difficult.
solution: Always create symlinks fresh on each machine with native paths rather than copying them. On macOS/Linux use 'ln -s /path/to/target /path/to/link'. On Windows, use 'cmd //c mklink /d <link> <target>' instead of Git Bash ln -s, which creates copies instead of real symlinks. Gitignore all symlinks since they are machine-specific. When debugging 'works locally but not on another machine', check symlinks first with 'file <path>' or 'readlink <path>'.
error_signature: silent 404 from asset server (Vite, webpack, etc) when symlinked path does not exist on current OS; no console error; works on one machine but not another

problem: When using vite-plugin-pwa or Workbox for service workers, the default precacheAndRoute(__WB_MANIFEST) automatically caches the entire build output. If the service worker's primary purpose is push notifications (not offline support), this causes stale-bundle problems: users' browsers serve cached old code even after new deployments, because the new service worker activates in the background but the page already loaded stale assets. Users clearing browser cache or doing hard-refresh does not fix it.
solution: For push-notification-only service workers: (1) Do NOT call precacheAndRoute, precache*, or any cache-related Workbox strategies. (2) If precaching must be used, implement an explicit activate event listener that deletes all prior-version caches by name (default Workbox cleanup only handles its own naming). (3) Audit any new vite-plugin-pwa, workbox-*, or service worker setup for hidden precache defaults before deploying. (4) If strange deployment issues occur (frontend changes don't reach users after hard-refresh), check the service worker's cache behavior first, not deployment status.
error_signature: Frontend code changes not visible to users after deployment even with Ctrl+Shift+R or hard-refresh; new service worker activates in background but page already loaded stale cached bundle

problem: When deleting large code blocks (>100 lines) or entire files, a single grep showing no hits can be misleading. Dead-code detection based on one grep search risks breaking live code that depends on the target through indirect imports, dynamic references, or activation via environment variables.
solution: Before deleting code larger than ~100 lines, run at least three independent grep searches on different symbols converging to 'zero hits outside the target': (1) grep exported function/class names from the file or function; (2) grep the import path that would reach it; (3) grep any environment variables or config keys that would activate the module. Only proceed with deletion if all three searches return zero real hits. For function-level deletes >50 lines, also grep unique string literals (log messages, error text) the function uses. If any signal shows a hit, the deletion is unsafe. Rationale: one grep is a hypothesis; three independent converging signals constitute evidence.
error_signature: accidentally deleted active code that had zero direct imports but was activated by env vars or dynamic references

problem: When removing unused imports from Vue SFC (.vue) files during refactoring, it is easy to miss that an import is still in use (especially in templates or code paths not immediately visible), causing runtime blank screens. Tree-shaking does not catch these at build time because the symbol is referenced at runtime. Manual inspection via grep or eyeballing is unreliable.
solution: Always run a typecheck before committing import removals from .vue files. Use 'npx vue-tsc --noEmit' (optionally scoped to the changed file) to catch unused-import removals that leave dangling references. If tsconfig emits baseUrl deprecation warnings, pass '--ignoreDeprecations 6.0'. For faster iteration during refactoring, run 'vue-tsc --watch' in a separate terminal and watch for type errors to clear as you remove imports. Do not commit until typecheck returns zero errors.
error_signature: Vue SFC blank screen after removing import; symbol still referenced in template or non-obvious code path

problem: When a generic enum-dispatching handler and a type-specific handler both exist for the same operation (e.g., DELETE), the type-specific handler gets updated with new cascading logic while the generic handler becomes stale. Since the UI often routes through the generic handler for uniformity, the cascading deletes are silently skipped, leaving orphaned downstream state (webhooks, installs, overrides) still active. Example: deleting a Forge automation type-specifically hard-deletes both creation and subscriptions, but the generic DELETE /creations/{id} handler only soft-archives and misses the webhook cleanup, so stale notifications continue firing.
solution: Audit both the type-specific AND generic dispatcher paths when adding a new enum-dispatched creation type with downstream state. (1) Grep for all deletion paths across the codebase (type-specific routes + generic dispatcher branches). (2) Run a DB-level orphan check with a join query to verify no downstream entities remain active after parent deletion, e.g., 'SELECT * FROM downstream_table WHERE parent_id=X AND is_active=1'. (3) For generic if/elif/else dispatchers, enforce a contract: either use a typecheck that prevents adding new types without updating the dispatcher, or add a default-deny else branch that raises an error. (4) Prefer consolidating type-specific handlers into a single generic branch with type filtering (e.g., if type in (widget, app, studio)) rather than duplicating cascade logic across multiple routes.
error_signature: Orphaned downstream entities (webhooks still firing, installations still active) after parent creation deleted via generic dispatcher; type-specific handler has correct cleanup but generic handler is stale

problem: Hardcoded user_id="default" fallback in function signatures, Pydantic fields, or query parameters creates a shared data bucket vulnerability. Multiple users' data converges on one 'default' record, or allows caller control via ?user_id=victim. This pattern keeps reappearing in new code despite remediation because AI agents pattern-match against existing code.
solution: Always route user_id through a dependency-injection helper instead of hardcoding a default. In FastAPI/Starlette: use `user_id: str = Depends(resolve_user_id)` where resolve_user_id is a function that extracts the authenticated user's ID from the request context (e.g. from session, JWT, or request object). Never write `user_id: str = "default"` in function signatures, Pydantic model defaults, or Query/Path parameters. If modifying existing code with hardcoded defaults, replace them even if not flagged by audit.
error_signature: data isolation violation: multiple users writing to same record; or attacker-controlled user_id query parameter leaks another user's data

problem: When documentation cites enumerable constants (arrays, lists, enums, regex patterns) defined in source code, paraphrasing or re-typing from memory causes silent omissions. The dropped items only surface later when the code enforces the constant and rejects something the docs claimed was allowed, creating a docs-vs-code contradiction that wastes debugging time.
solution: Always copy enumerable constants verbatim from their source file into documentation. Include a line-number reference (e.g., 'tokens-lint.mjs:9' or 'tokens-lint.mjs#L9') so readers can verify instantly. During docs review, use grep or direct file inspection to compare the prose enumeration against the actual constant definition; a length mismatch or item count difference signals a paraphrase drop. In metaprompts that delegate docs authoring involving script constants, add explicit instruction: 'copy the array/list verbatim from the source file — do not re-type or summarize'.
error_signature: docs cite script constant but omit items; code enforcer rejects what docs claimed was allowed; grep -n shows constant in source longer than prose enumeration

problem: When an LLM generates specific quantitative claims (contrast ratios, color math, percentage calculations, version-bump results, token counts), accepting the number on LLM authority alone -- even when multiple LLM runs agree -- can result in incorrect values shipped to production. The failure mode is especially common when generating structurally-similar outputs in one pass (multiple components, multiple tables): the LLM reaches for 'plausible-looking' numbers from its recent context window rather than recomputing, causing cargo-culted values and copy-paste errors across files.
solution: For any quantitative claim outside a directly-readable source file, write a deterministic throwaway script (30 lines or less) that computes the correct value. For WCAG contrast ratios, use the standard luminance formula: convert hex to RGB, apply the sRGB companding function (s <= 0.03928 ? s/12.92 : ((s+0.055)/1.055)^2.4 for each channel), compute relative luminance as 0.2126*R + 0.7152*G + 0.0722*B, then contrast ratio = (L_light + 0.05) / (L_dark + 0.05). Run the script against the disputed values, accept only the script output as authoritative, and delete the script after fix commit. Do not accept LLM-vs-LLM agreement on math as sufficient verification.
error_signature: LLM generates contrast ratio claim (e.g., '4.5:1') that differs from actual computed value (e.g., 7.35:1); multiple LLM outputs agree on the wrong number; values copy-pasted between similar contexts in one session

problem: Docker layer caching prevents fresh clones of dependencies when only the dependency repo is updated. Changing a git-cloned dependency and pushing to its repo does not trigger a rebuild if the Docker RUN command is unchanged. This can lead to confusing runtime errors (e.g. ImportError) that appear to be code bugs but are actually caused by stale cached git clones.
solution: Use a Docker cache-busting argument (ARG CACHE_BUST=vN-description) in the Dockerfile RUN command that clones dependencies. When dependencies are updated, increment this argument value and rebuild the Docker image. If using a remote build system (e.g. Railway), set the CACHE_BUST value as an environment variable override on the remote system, as the remote system may not pick up Dockerfile changes automatically from git. For Dockerfile-only changes, explicitly upload the updated Dockerfile to the build system (e.g. via 'railway up --detach') before updating environment variables, since pushing to git alone may not trigger the remote system to use the new Dockerfile.
error_signature: Docker layer cache reuse with git clone; stale dependency version at runtime despite pushing to dependency repo; ImportError or similar import failures that disappear after cache invalidation

problem: Python urllib HTTP requests to a Cloudflare-protected backend return 403 Forbidden, while curl requests to the same endpoint succeed. The issue occurs when making POST requests from scripts (e.g., a daemon pushing notifications).
solution: Add a User-Agent header to all urllib requests. Cloudflare blocks requests that lack or have a generic User-Agent. Set a descriptive User-Agent string such as 'MyScript/1.0' in the request headers. Example: import urllib.request; req = urllib.request.Request(url, data=data, headers={'User-Agent': 'MyScript/1.0'}); response = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
error_signature: urllib POST returns 403 Forbidden to Cloudflare-protected endpoint

problem: When deploying a Python application with Coinbase CDP x402 payment middleware to a production environment, the SDK fails with 'ModuleNotFoundError: No module named x402' even after installation, or fails with 'ValueError: Facilitator get_supported failed (401)' when targeting mainnet.
solution: Two fixes required: (1) Verify which Python virtual environment the application launcher/entrypoint actually uses -- if multiple venvs exist in the project, ensure x402 is installed in the correct one (e.g. if a LaunchAgent or systemd service points to a specific venv path, install there). (2) For mainnet x402 facilitators that require authentication (e.g. Coinbase CDP api.cdp.coinbase.com/platform/v2/x402), use cdp.x402.create_facilitator_config(api_key, api_secret) instead of FacilitatorConfig(url=...) without auth. For testnet/open facilitators, the unauthenticated url-only path works. Always verify your environment variables (API_KEY, API_SECRET) are set and valid by testing get_supported() before deploying.
error_signature: ModuleNotFoundError x402 | ValueError Facilitator get_supported failed 401

problem: Moonshot Kimi K2/K2.5 API has several non-obvious constraints: temperature parameter only accepts exactly 1 (rejects 0.7, 0.5, etc); reasoning output goes to separate reasoning_content field instead of content; when max_tokens budget exhausts during thinking phase, content field returns empty string instead of error; API endpoint must be api.moonshot.ai not api.moonshot.cn
solution: When integrating Moonshot Kimi models: (1) Always set temperature=1, do not attempt other values. (2) Check both response.choices[0].message.content AND response.choices[0].message.reasoning_content; reasoning_content contains the thinking trace. (3) If content is empty, fall back to reasoning_content as the answer -- this happens when max_tokens budget runs out during thinking, not an error condition. (4) Use base_url='https://api.moonshot.ai/v1' not .cn domain. Example: client = OpenAI(base_url='https://api.moonshot.ai/v1', api_key=key); answer = response.choices[0].message.content or response.choices[0].message.reasoning_content
error_signature: temperature parameter rejected; empty content field with non-empty reasoning_content; connection failure with .cn domain

problem: Electron app (Windows NSIS installer) CI release pipeline via GitHub Actions is consistently failing at startup (0s, workflow file issue). Local release build with code-signing, GitHub Release asset upload, and auto-update manifest generation works reliably when run manually with proper environment setup.
solution: For Electron + electron-builder + Windows code-signing releases: skip the broken CI workflow and publish locally. (1) Bump version with `npm version patch -m "release: v%s -- <message>"` and push tags. (2) Set GH_TOKEN environment variable with `export GH_TOKEN="$(gh auth token)"`. (3) Run `npm run publish` locally (triggers electron-vite build, electron-builder for Windows NSIS, and publishes to GitHub Releases). (4) Verify the release includes: signed .exe installer, latest.yml (auto-update manifest), and .blockmap file — all three are required for auto-update to function. Code-signing works locally with proper Azure credential profiles configured in the build environment; earlier belief that signing only works in CI was incorrect. Auto-update manifest verification: `gh release view vTAG --repo OWNER/REPO --json assets` must list latest.yml.
error_signature: GitHub Actions release workflow startup_failure at 0s -- workflow file issue suspected (exhausted Windows Actions minutes or token scope limitation)

problem: When submitting an ow-electron application built with @overwolf/ow-electron and ow-electron-builder to Overwolf store, developer may incorrectly prepare submission artifact as a manually-zipped .opk file (similar to native CEF apps), causing upload failure or rejection.
solution: For ow-electron apps, submit the SIGNED NSIS .exe setup file directly, not a hand-zipped .opk. The build process (npm run package:ow) generates the final artifact as dist/AppName-<version>-OW-Setup.exe, already signed and with all Overwolf packages bundled (confirmed by presence of owutility.dll in win-unpacked/). Upload via console.overwolf.com (drag .exe into draft release) or ow-cli: ow-cli electron upload ./path/to/version.exe -aid <appId> -w. Overwolf wraps the .exe automatically. Do NOT attempt to create a separate .opk archive. All Overwolf configuration (GEP packages, overlay settings) is defined in package.json (overwolf.packages array and optional build.overwolf section), not a separate manifest.json file.
error_signature: overwolf submission .opk not found / invalid artifact format ow-electron

problem: Python sqlite3.Connection.executescript() silently issues an implicit COMMIT before executing the SQL script. If any statement in a multi-statement script fails, earlier statements are already permanently committed and cannot be rolled back. This causes migration runners to leave databases in a half-applied state after the first error.
solution: Never use executescript() for migrations. Instead: (1) Split SQL into individual statements. (2) Run under explicit 'BEGIN EXCLUSIVE; ... COMMIT;' using conn.execute() for each statement. (3) Catch (sqlite3.Error, OSError) and call conn.execute('ROLLBACK') in a defensive nested try-block (ROLLBACK itself can fail if no transaction was started). Additionally, set isolation_level=None when opening the connection to enable manual transaction mode and prevent Python's auto-begin from conflicting with explicit BEGIN EXCLUSIVE.
error_signature: sqlite3.Error during migration; earlier statements already committed; rollback() is no-op; database left in half-applied state

problem: When deploying a service on Railway that needs to reach another internal service via `<service>.railway.internal` hostname, the connection fails with NameResolutionError or DNS resolution errors if the two services are in different Railway projects. The failure often surfaces indirectly as a runtime error (e.g. token verification failure, database connection error) rather than an obvious DNS error, making diagnosis difficult.
solution: Railway internal DNS (`*.railway.internal`) only resolves within the same project. Cross-project service-to-service communication via internal DNS is not supported. To fix: (1) Colocate both services in the same Railway project (recommended -- services remain isolated in separate containers with independent deploy cycles), (2) Expose the target service publicly with proper authentication (e.g. API key) and reference its public URL instead, or (3) Use Railway Private Networking if on a paid tier. To diagnose: SSH into the consuming container and run `nslookup <service>.railway.internal` -- if it returns NXDOMAIN, the target service is in a different project.
error_signature: NameResolutionError: Name or service not known | nslookup returns NXDOMAIN for *.railway.internal

problem: When running SQLite queries with json_extract() through SSH tunneling via bash, the dollar-sign $ in JSON path expressions like '$.key' gets expanded by the shell, causing silent failures (empty results instead of errors). The escaping required differs from direct sqlite3 calls.
solution: When dispatching SQLite commands through SSH with double-quoting, escape the dollar sign in json_extract paths with three backslashes before the dollar: '\\$.key'. Example: railway ssh "sqlite3 /path/to/db.db \"SELECT json_extract(data, '\\$.subscription_id') FROM table;\"" — the three backslashes become one literal backslash-dollar in the final sqlite3 argument, preventing shell expansion at each layer (host shell, SSH quoting, and sqlite3 argv parsing).
error_signature: json_extract(col, '$.key') returns empty/null silently when used in ssh-dispatched sqlite3 queries instead of error or expected result

problem: When testing email forwarding with Cloudflare Email Routing, sending from the same Gmail account that receives the forwarded mail results in a false negative -- the message appears not to arrive, but it actually does and gets deduplicated by Gmail's Message-ID collision detection (sender and final recipient are identical).
solution: To properly test Cloudflare Email Routing forwarding, send the test email from a DIFFERENT email address (different Gmail account, external email provider, or any address other than the final recipient). Do NOT send from the destination inbox back to itself. For example: send from a second Gmail account or colleague's email to your alias at the domain, then verify arrival in the destination inbox. Self-loop tests will appear to fail but are actually succeeding silently.
error_signature: Cloudflare Email Routing alias forward appears broken; sent test message to alias but received nothing in destination inbox

problem: Elegoo Centauri Carbon 1 (CC1) printer: WebSocket-based SDCP protocol control for 3D printing over local network. Key gotchas: (1) SDCP command Cmd 402 (bed leveling) can wedge the printer's network stack if called in rapid-fire loops or with concurrent connections -- all ports refuse and touchscreen goes blank, requiring power-cycle. (2) Cmd 403 (set temperatures) payload format was unverified until confirmed. (3) OrcaSlicer CLI requires separate --load-settings and --load-filaments flags (not a single merged config), uses semicolon-separated paths for settings, and hardcodes output to plate_1.gcode. (4) Large gcode files (>1-2 MB) fail over single HTTP POST due to embedded server limits.
solution: When controlling Elegoo CC1 over SDCP WebSocket (ws://PRINTER_IP:3030/websocket): (a) Never fire commands in tight loops or spawn concurrent connections -- the WS server appears single-client and will reject or hang. Add delays between SDCP calls (e.g., 0.5-1s min between commands). (b) Avoid casual calls to Cmd 402 (EDIT_PRINTER_AXIS_ZERO / home+level) -- it triggers a 3-5 minute leveling routine that makes the WS unreachable and can lock up the network stack if combined with rapid retries. (c) For Cmd 403 (EDIT_PRINTER_STATUS_DATA), use these verified payload keys: TempTargetHotbed (0-110), TempTargetNozzle (0-280), TempTargetBox (0-60), LightStatus with SecondLight and RgbLight array. (d) When slicing with OrcaSlicer CLI, use THREE separate flags: --load-settings "machine.json;process.json" and --load-filaments "filament.json" (not one merged config or --load). Settings paths are semicolon-delimited within the single --load-settings arg. (e) For gcode files larger than ~1-2 MB, upload in chunks via multipart or custom chunked protocol rather than a single POST, as the printer's embedded HTTP server will reset on large payloads. Always rename output from the hardcoded plate_1.gcode after slicing.
error_signature: network stack wedge on rapid SDCP calls; http 413 or connection reset on large gcode upload; invalid --load flag in orca-slicer.exe cli
